refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work performed.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from correct design and operation of subsystems, not a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution can continuously or periodically capture energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and work_performed (tons moved or equivalent work units), compute energy_per_ton, and expose this metric via operator interface, logs, or telemetry system. Verify accuracy against calibrated instrumentation over a representative evaluation period.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure or infer the quantity of work performed (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or equivalent work unit).
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute energy efficiency as energy consumed per unit work performed.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide visibility of energy efficiency metrics to authorized stakeholders (operator, site manager, maintenance).

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument the energy source (fuel/power input) with continuous measurement and logging; instrument the work output (tonnage moved) with independent measurement and logging; compute energy_per_ton as a derived metric post-collection.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and work sensors into the equipment control loop; calculate energy_per_ton continuously during operation and store snapshots at defined intervals.

DP3: Establish a dual-channel measurement architecture: (A) energy metering at the power distribution point (centralized), (B) work quantification via independent load/displacement sensors; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio offline.
DP4: Integrate energy and work measurement into a unified telemetry module that fuses sensor inputs, applies calibration corrections, and outputs energy_per_ton with uncertainty bounds in real-time.

DP5: Separate measurement into two independent subsystems: (A) energy accounting via utility/fuel consumption records and equipment-level power draw, (B) work accounting via task-completion logs and payload tracking; reconcile via a reporting aggregation layer.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Establish a dual-channel measurement architecture: (A) energy metering at the power distribution point (centralized), (B) work quantification via independent load/displacement sensors; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio offline.

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization drift between energy and work channels could introduce systematic error if not actively managed.
Offline computation introduces latency; if real-time feedback is later required, DP3 may require refactoring to DP4.
Sensor calibration and maintenance procedures must be coordinated across two independent subsystems to avoid divergence.
Data pipeline assumes reliable timestamp correlation; loss of synchronization events could create gaps in energy_per_ton calculation.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves strong logical independence by separating energy measurement (power domain) from work measurement (mechanical domain), reducing cross-domain coupling. Each channel can be designed, tested, and maintained independently. The synchronization requirement is a known interface contract, not a hidden dependency. Offline computation avoids tight real-time coupling but trades latency for simplicity and auditability. This design supports modular IM packaging (separate energy module, separate work module, separate reporting module) and allows substitution of sensor technologies without affecting the logical architecture.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is metered at a single, well-defined point (e.g., fuel tank, power panel). RISK: If energy is distributed across multiple sources or if parasitic losses are significant, centralized metering may not capture true system energy consumption. Mitigation: Validate energy boundary definition with stakeholders; consider secondary loss accounting if needed.

ASSUMPTION: Work output (tonnage moved) can be independently quantified without relying on energy-side instrumentation. RISK: If load cells or displacement sensors are absent or unreliable, work measurement fails independently. Mitigation: Conduct sensor redundancy analysis; establish acceptance criteria for sensor accuracy before deployment.

ASSUMPTION: Timestamp synchronization can be achieved to within acceptable tolerance (e.g., ±1 second or better). RISK: Clock drift or network latency in distributed systems could introduce systematic bias. Mitigation: Implement NTP or GPS-based time synchronization; log synchronization quality metrics.

ASSUMPTION: Offline computation is acceptable for reporting; real-time feedback is not a hard requirement. RISK: If operational decisions must be made in-loop based on energy_per_ton, latency becomes a functional constraint. Mitigation: Clarify real-time vs. post-hoc reporting requirements early; design IM to support both if needed.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough to average out transient effects and sensor noise. RISK: Short evaluation windows or high variability in work conditions could make energy_per_ton unstable. Mitigation: Define minimum evaluation period and acceptable variance bounds in acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: No significant energy is consumed by measurement and reporting infrastructure itself. RISK: If instrumentation power draw is non-negligible, it must be accounted for or excluded from energy_per_ton calculation. Mitigation: Quantify instrumentation overhead; document inclusion/exclusion in metric definition.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable continuous measurement of energy consumption and work performed, with synchronized data collection and post-processing to compute and report energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved. The measurement architecture shall separate energy metering (at the power source) from work quantification (via load/displacement sensors), with timestamp synchronization to within [±T_sync] and offline aggregation to support reporting against E_target over the agreed evaluation period. Instrumentation overhead shall be [excluded from | accounted separately in] the energy_per_ton calculation. Real-time feedback [is | is not] required.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement architecture encompassing dual-channel data acquisition (energy metering at power distribution point + work quantification via load/displacement sensors), timestamp synchronization logic, and offline ratio computation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit synchronized dual-channel measurements (energy, work, timestamps) and computed efficiency ratios to external monitoring/analysis systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive measurement configuration directives (sensor calibration, sampling rates, synchronization parameters) from task controller

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicate measurement data and configuration commands across distributed nodes if sensors are geographically separated

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of energy/work measurement and efficiency analysis. Centralized power metering and sensor fusion are core to NGHE's functional scope. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry (output) and Tasking (input) cross boundaries; timestamp synchronization and offline ratio computation remain fully internal.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns mechanical/kinematic state and actuation. Dual-channel measurement architecture (especially centralized energy metering) is orthogonal to vehicle dynamics. High diffusion: energy metering logic, sensor fusion, and ratio computation would leak into Vehicle, violating separation of concerns. Vehicle should consume measurement outputs, not own measurement infrastructure.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation owns command/feedback loops for remote control. Measurement architecture is independent of teleoperation protocol. Placing dual-channel metering here creates high coupling and violates cohesion: teleoperation logic and measurement fusion are unrelated concerns. Measurement data should flow to Teleoperation as input, not originate from it.

Poor fit: Safety Services owns hazard detection and SafetyAlert generation. While efficiency ratios may inform safety thresholds, the measurement architecture itself (dual-channel metering, sensor fusion, timestamp sync) is not a safety function. Placing measurement infrastructure here conflates measurement with safety logic, creating high diffusion and poor cohesion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The dual-channel measurement architecture (DP3) is fundamentally a measurement and efficiency quantification concern, which is the core responsibility of the NGHE Solution module. Centralizing energy metering at the power distribution point and fusing it with independent work sensors via timestamp synchronization and offline ratio computation are all internal to NGHE's domain. External interfaces (Telemetry for output, Tasking for configuration, Network Link for distributed coordination) are minimal and well-defined, indicating strong containment.
containment_risks: If Vehicle module owns its own load/displacement sensors independently, sensor data must be marshaled into NGHE Solution for fusion; ensure clear data ownership and avoid duplicate sensor reading logic in Vehicle.

If Teleoperation requires real-time efficiency feedback for operator guidance, latency of offline ratio computation may necessitate a secondary fast-path; ensure this does not fragment measurement logic across modules.
If Safety Services needs to monitor energy/work ratios for anomaly detection, establish a clean Telemetry subscription interface rather than embedding measurement logic in Safety Services.

Timestamp synchronization across distributed sensors (if Vehicle and NGHE Solution are physically separated) requires robust Network Link coordination; ensure synchronization protocol is owned by NGHE Solution, not delegated to Vehicle or Teleoperation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly and sufficiently addresses FR requirement. The dual-channel architecture (centralized energy metering + independent work quantification) with timestamp synchronization and offline ratio computation is a sound logical solution for measuring energy consumption per unit of work. The DP is technology-neutral, decomposable, and testable against the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target).

diffusion_risks: Vehicle module independence: If Vehicle owns load/displacement sensors and reads them autonomously, sensor data marshaling into NGHE Solution must be formalized via a clean interface contract. Risk: duplicate sensor reading logic or inconsistent calibration across modules.

Real-time efficiency feedback path: Offline ratio computation may create latency. If Teleoperation or Safety Services require sub-second efficiency feedback for operator guidance or anomaly detection, a secondary fast-path computation may emerge outside NGHE Solution. Risk: measurement logic fragmentation across modules.

Distributed timestamp synchronization: If energy metering (power distribution point) and work sensors (Vehicle-mounted) are geographically separated, synchronization protocol ownership must remain with NGHE Solution. Risk: if Vehicle or Network Link module owns sync logic, DP coherence is lost.

Safety Services subscription: If Safety Services monitors efficiency ratios for threshold-based alerts, establish a unidirectional Telemetry subscription interface. Risk: embedding measurement thresholds or ratio computation in Safety Services violates separation of concerns.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. Ownership is well-justified: dual-channel measurement, sensor fusion, and efficiency ratio computation are core to NGHE's functional scope. External interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, Network Link inout) are minimal and well-defined. All internal logic (timestamp sync, offline ratio computation) remains within NGHE boundary. No competing module has stronger functional claim. Recommended: formalize interface contracts for sensor data ingestion (from Vehicle), configuration directives (from Tasking), and telemetry output (to external systems) to prevent diffusion.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable from DP3 outputs: energy_used (from centralized power metering) and ton_moved (from work quantification sensors). Offline ratio computation allows rigorous post-evaluation analysis over agreed evaluation period. Timestamp synchronization ensures data coherence. No hidden dependencies or unmeasurable intermediate states.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the sole owner of measurement architecture and efficiency computation. Vehicle provides work sensor inputs (load/displacement) via defined interface. Tasking provides configuration inputs (calibration, sampling rates, sync parameters). Telemetry consumes measurement outputs. Safety Services and Teleoperation consume efficiency ratios as read-only telemetry, not as owners. Ownership boundaries are unambiguous and non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Formalize sensor data interface contract: Define schema, sampling rate, calibration metadata, and error handling for load/displacement sensor data flowing from Vehicle to NGHE Solution. Specify ownership of sensor calibration logic (recommend: NGHE Solution owns calibration, Vehicle owns raw sensor reading).

Document timestamp synchronization protocol: Specify clock source, synchronization frequency, tolerance bounds, and fallback behavior if sync fails. Assign protocol ownership explicitly to NGHE Solution.

Define Tasking configuration interface: Specify which parameters (sensor calibration, sampling rates, sync parameters) are configurable at runtime vs. fixed at deployment. Establish validation rules in NGHE Solution to reject invalid configurations.

Establish Telemetry subscription model: Define which consumers (Safety Services, Teleoperation, external monitoring) subscribe to which telemetry streams (raw measurements vs. computed ratios). Clarify latency expectations and update frequency for each consumer.

Address offline vs. real-time computation trade-off: If real-time efficiency feedback is required by any consumer, document the fast-path computation logic separately from offline ratio computation. Ensure both paths use identical algorithms to avoid divergence.

Specify Network Link coordination for distributed sensors: If sensors are geographically separated, define message format, retry logic, and clock synchronization mechanism. Ensure NGHE Solution owns the coordination logic, not Vehicle or Network Link module.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. DP3 is logically sound, architecturally well-contained, and directly verifiable against the acceptance criterion. However, the gate results expose six critical diffusion risks and interface formalization gaps that must be resolved through next-level decomposition. Given high safety criticality, the recommended revisions are not cosmetic: they require explicit sub-FRs to govern sensor data ingestion, timestamp synchronization, configuration management, and telemetry subscription. Decomposing now ensures these interface contracts and ownership boundaries are designed rigorously rather than left as implementation details. This is a controlled zigzag: we proceed downward to decompose DP3 into sub-DPs that address each diffusion risk and formalize the interface contracts flagged in the gate review.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Ingest and validate work sensor data (load and displacement) from Vehicle module with defined schema, sampling rate, calibration metadata, and error handling.
FR3.2: Synchronize timestamps across centralized energy metering and distributed work sensors to ensure coherent energy-to-work ratio computation.
FR3.3: Accept and validate runtime configuration directives (sensor calibration parameters, sampling rates, synchronization parameters) from Tasking module.
FR3.4: Compute energy-per-unit-work ratio offline using synchronized energy and work measurements, with configurable evaluation window and aggregation logic.

FR3.5: Publish efficiency telemetry (raw measurements and computed ratios) to authorized consumers (Safety Services, Teleoperation, external monitoring) with defined latency and update frequency guarantees.
FR3.6: Detect and handle synchronization failures, sensor data gaps, and configuration errors with fallback behavior and diagnostic logging.

revise.why: DP3 is logically correct but underspecified at the interface level. The six sub-FRs above will drive decomposition of DP3 into six sub-DPs that explicitly address: (1) sensor data contract and validation logic, (2) timestamp synchronization protocol and ownership, (3) configuration interface and validation rules, (4) offline ratio computation algorithm and evaluation window, (5) telemetry subscription model and latency SLAs, and (6) error handling and fallback strategies. This decomposition will eliminate the diffusion risks identified in the DP-IM gate and formalize the interface contracts recommended in the gate review. The revised DP3 will be a composite design that clearly delineates internal logic (owned by NGHE Solution) from external interfaces (owned by Vehicle, Tasking, Safety Services, Teleoperation, Network Link).

assumptions: Vehicle module owns raw sensor reading logic; NGHE Solution owns sensor calibration and data validation logic.
Timestamp synchronization is feasible with sub-second accuracy across geographically distributed sensors via Network Link.
Offline ratio computation is acceptable for efficiency measurement; real-time feedback (if required) will be addressed as a secondary fast-path in sub-DP4.
Safety Services and Teleoperation consume efficiency ratios as read-only telemetry; they do not own measurement or computation logic.
Tasking module is the sole source of runtime configuration directives; configuration changes are validated by NGHE Solution before application.
All sensor data, timestamps, and computed ratios are logged for post-evaluation analysis and audit trail.
open_risks: Sensor calibration ownership: If Vehicle module resists transferring calibration logic to NGHE Solution, sensor data quality and consistency may degrade. Mitigation: establish clear interface contract specifying that NGHE Solution owns calibration parameters and validation rules; Vehicle provides only raw sensor readings.

Timestamp synchronization latency: If clock synchronization frequency is too low or tolerance bounds are too loose, energy-to-work ratio computation may be inaccurate. Mitigation: sub-FR3.2 will specify synchronization protocol with measurable SLAs; testing will validate accuracy over extended evaluation periods.

Real-time efficiency feedback: If Safety Services or Teleoperation require sub-second efficiency feedback for operator guidance or anomaly detection, offline ratio computation may be insufficient. Mitigation: sub-FR3.5 will clarify latency expectations; if real-time feedback is required, sub-DP4 will include a secondary fast-path computation with identical algorithms to offline path.

Configuration validation: If Tasking module sends invalid configuration directives (e.g., out-of-range calibration parameters), NGHE Solution must reject them gracefully. Mitigation: sub-FR3.3 will specify validation rules and error handling; testing will cover invalid configuration scenarios.

Telemetry subscription scalability: If many consumers subscribe to telemetry streams, message throughput and latency may become bottlenecks. Mitigation: sub-FR3.5 will define subscription model with explicit latency and update frequency guarantees; architecture will support filtering and aggregation to reduce message volume.

Safety-critical measurement integrity: Given high safety criticality, any measurement error or data loss could affect safety decisions. Mitigation: sub-FR3.6 will specify error detection, logging, and fallback behavior; testing will include failure scenarios (sensor loss, sync failure, configuration error) to verify safe degradation.
evidence_needed: Sensor data interface specification: Schema, sampling rate, calibration metadata format, error codes, and validation rules for load/displacement sensor data from Vehicle.
Timestamp synchronization protocol specification: Clock source, synchronization frequency, tolerance bounds, fallback behavior, and ownership assignment.
Configuration interface specification: List of configurable parameters, valid ranges, validation rules, and error handling for Tasking directives.
Telemetry subscription model specification: Consumer list, subscribed streams (raw measurements vs. computed ratios), latency SLAs, and update frequency for each consumer.
Offline vs. real-time computation trade-off analysis: If real-time feedback is required, document fast-path computation logic and algorithm equivalence proof.
Network Link coordination specification: Message format, retry logic, clock synchronization mechanism, and ownership assignment for distributed sensor coordination.
Error handling and fallback behavior specification: Failure scenarios (sensor loss, sync failure, configuration error), detection logic, logging, and safe degradation strategy.
Testing plan for interface contracts: Unit tests for sensor data validation, timestamp synchronization, configuration validation, telemetry subscription, and error handling.
